retrieved_docs.content: Molecular studies: PCR and NGS of BMA cells can detect gene mutations that are important for diagnosis, risk stratification, and selection of targeted therapies in acute leukemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Quality Control and Lab Safety
retrieved_docs.content: Generally, 2 different concentrations of QC materials are required for effective statistical QC. For quantitative measurement procedures, it is important to select QC materials with analyte concentrations that reflect clinical decision values throughout the procedure's analytical measuring range. [89]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview on Ordering and Evaluation of Laboratory Tests -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)
retrieved_docs.content: It is an increased proliferation of the granulocytic cell line without loss of their differentiation ability. There is a leukoerythroblastic picture on peripheral blood film with a predominance of immature red cells and granulocytes. Diagnostic test for CML is the presence of bcr-abl fusion gene in more than 90% of patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Tumor Biomarkers -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Polymerase chain reaction (PCR): PCR is a technique that amplifies a specific DNA segment by cycling through denaturation, annealing of primers, and extension using a thermostable DNA polymerase. PCR is used to detect the bcr-abl1 fusion present in many forms of chronic myeloid leukemia. PCR is also used to detect microsatellite instability or mutations in genes like K- ras , N- ras , and BRAF , which have prognostic and predictive implications in colorectal cancer. [26] PCR is utilized to detect HER2 gene amplification, which helps identify patients who may benefit from HER2-targeted therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Initially, if CML is suspected, cytogenetic testing, fluorescent in situ hybridization, and/or reverse transcriptase-polymerase chain reaction (PCR) to determine the Philadelphia chromosome, or BCR-ABL1 oncoprotein presence can be performed on peripheral blood. At the time of diagnosis, laboratory blood testing should include a complete blood count with differential, chemistry panel, hepatitis panel, and a quantitative PCR for BCR-ABL1 . A baseline bone marrow aspirate and biopsy should be performed with cytogenetics. Quantitative PCR should be repeated every 3 months after initiation of therapy. After BCR-ABL1 is less than or equal to 1% (international scale), quantitative PCR should continue for 2 years and then every 3 to 6 months after 2 years. If chronic phase CML is established, additional evaluation includes determining the risk score using Sokal et al or Hasford et al risk calculations before determining first-line therapy. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Diagnostic Tests -- Minimal Residual Disease Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Polymerase chain reaction–based minimal residual disease monitoring: PCR is a highly sensitive method for detecting specific genetic abnormalities associated with leukemia at very low levels, enabling precise MRD detection. MRD positivity following induction therapy or during remission is linked to an increased risk of relapse, potentially guiding treatment intensification or consideration of stem cell transplantation. [66]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Quality Control and Lab Safety
retrieved_docs.content: The acceptable range and guidelines for interpreting QC results are determined by the likelihood of identifying analytical errors while minimizing false alerts. The standard method for expressing QC interpretive rules uses an abbreviation system, which is widely recognized in clinical laboratories through the work of Westgard. This nomenclature offers a concise and systematic way to communicate QC limits and criteria for identifying potential errors in laboratory analyses. [88]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Interfering Factors -- Sampling Error
retrieved_docs.content: BMA/BMB are invasive procedures that may not fully capture the entire spectrum of disease within the bone marrow. This can result in sampling bias and potential misinterpretation of the true disease burden.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Interfering Factors -- Interfering Substances
retrieved_docs.content: Certain medications, such as heparin and specific antibiotics, may interfere with flow cytometry analysis by causing nonspecific binding or altering antigen expression. Additionally, high levels of paraproteins, as seen in conditions such as multiple myeloma, can disrupt immunophenotypic analysis.

retrieved_docs.title: Tissue Evaluation for HER2 Tumor Marker -- Quality Control and Lab Safety
retrieved_docs.content: Lab accreditation holds immense significance in ensuring the quality and reliability of testing and measurement services. Further, accreditation formally recognizes the laboratory meets specific, internationally recognized standards of competence and compliance. [36] Optimal laboratory accreditation includes onsite inspection every other year with an annual requirement for self-inspection; reviews of laboratory validation, procedures, quality assurance results, processes, results, and reports; and unsatisfactory performance results in suspension of laboratory testing for HER2 for that method. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: The European Leukemia Net (ELN) 2013 criteria can be a guide to monitor failure or suboptimal response to first-line therapy or second-line therapy. These criteria evaluate patients with chronic phase CML at 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months and then any subsequent follow-up; the optimal response is defined by PCR as BCR-ABL1 transcripts (IS) of less than or equal to 10%, less than 1%, and less than or equal to 0.1%, respectively. Alternatively, if quantitative PCR is not available, cytogenetics can determine levels of the Philadelphia chromosome but is not useful in cryptic BCR-ABL1 . If the optimal response is not achieved, there is a warning response group and a failure response group. In the warning group, consideration of dose escalation of imatinib or changing tyrosine kinase inhibitors is warranted. [32] However, if an optimal response is not achieved at three months, studies show that most patients will achieve an optimal response at 6 months without changes in therapy with a similar prognosis. [33] Another consideration is patient compliance; poor adherence is a significant contributor to treatment failure and loss of complete response. [34] In high-risk cases, at the time of diagnosis or in case of treatment failure despite good patient compliance, the tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy should be changed. The patient should be evaluated for the possibility of allogeneic HSCT. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Quality Control and Lab Safety
retrieved_docs.content: Internal QC evaluates the accuracy of a measurement procedure by routinely testing a QC sample with a known expected result. When the result of the QC sample falls within the acceptable range of the expected value, it confirms that the testing procedure is functioning correctly. This ensures that patient sample results can be reported with high confidence, supporting their suitability for clinical use. Conversely, if the QC result falls outside acceptable limits, it signals that the testing system is not functioning properly, raising concerns about the reliability of patient sample results. In such cases, corrective action is required, and patient sample measurements may need to be repeated once the procedure has been restored to stable performance. A corrected report must be issued if inaccurate results were reported before identifying the error. [85] [86]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: While laboratory tests are crucial for diagnosing and managing acute leukemia, various factors can affect the accuracy and interpretation of results, potentially leading to misdiagnosis or improper management.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Bone Marrow Aspiration and Biopsy Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Molecular studies: The results of PCR or NGS assays are reported, specifying the presence or absence of identified gene mutations or fusion genes. The mutant allele burden is quantified if applicable.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Interfering Factors -- Genetic and Epigenetic Heterogeneity
retrieved_docs.content: Acute leukemia is characterized by significant genetic and epigenetic heterogeneity, with different subtypes and clones exhibiting distinct molecular profiles. This heterogeneity can pose challenges in interpreting molecular test results and identifying relevant therapeutic targets. [71]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1503
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib anhydrous is classified as a BCR‑ABL inhibitor, a pharmacologic agent that specifically targets the BCR‑ABL fusion proteins (bcr‑abl fúzové proteiny). By inhibiting these oncogenic fusion proteins, dasatinib anhydrous interferes with the constitutive tyrosine kinase activity that drives malignant cell proliferation in diseases such as chronic myeloid leukemia, making it a key component of targeted therapy strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib, also known as imatinib mesylate, is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor; it belongs to the class of agents that target the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase and is used therapeutically to inhibit this oncogenic driver in relevant hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor, meaning it belongs to the class of drugs that specifically target BCR‑ABL fusion proteins. BCR‑ABL fusion proteins serve as the molecular target for BCR‑ABL inhibitors; they are oncogenic drivers created by the Philadelphia chromosome translocation and are central to the pathogenesis of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By binding to and inhibiting these fusion proteins, imatinib and other BCR‑ABL inhibitors can suppress the abnormal signaling that promotes uncontrolled cell proliferation, thereby providing an effective targeted therapy for patients with these malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2646
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and dasatinib anhydrous are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a class of targeted therapies that block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase activity implicated in certain leukemias. By inhibiting this oncogenic kinase, both the standard and anhydrous formulations of dasatinib help to control malignant cell proliferation and are used in the management of chronic myeloid leukemia and Philadelphia chromosome‑positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Recognizing dasatinib and its anhydrous form as BCR‑ABL inhibitors underscores their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in precision oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1361
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology is a classification framework that encompasses a diverse set of biomarker concepts, including the bcr‑abl fusion proteins, human factor VIII, and the sex steroid hormones. By grouping these distinct molecular entities—oncogenic fusion proteins (bcr‑abl), a coagulation factor critical for hemostasis (factor VIII), and endogenous steroid hormones that regulate reproductive and metabolic functions—CTRP Biomarker Terminology provides a structured reference for researchers and clinicians to identify and discuss biomarkers across oncology, hematology, and endocrinology. This inclusive categorization facilitates interdisciplinary communication and supports integrated approaches to diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic decision‑making that involve these varied biomarker types.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2743
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is employed as a descriptor indicating a nedostatek (deficiency) of bcr-abl fúzové proteiny (BCR‑ABL fusion proteins) and also a nedostatek of síra (sulfur). This usage shows that the qualifier can be applied both to molecular abnormalities, such as the absence of oncogenic BCR‑ABL fusion proteins, and to elemental deficiencies, such as a lack of sulfur, providing a consistent way to denote missing or insufficient entities in medical documentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: BCR-ABL Inhibitor is a class of targeted therapy that includes ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib. Ponatinib and its hydrochloride form, as well as asciminib, are members of the BCR-ABL Inhibitor class and are employed in the treatment of أبيضاض الدم النقوي, a myeloid leukemia. These agents act by inhibiting the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase, thereby controlling disease progression. Their shared mechanism and therapeutic indication underscore the importance of BCR‑ABL inhibition in managing this hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2917
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and imatinib mesylát are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي, a form of lymphoid leukemia. Both drugs target abnormal tyrosine‑kinase activity characteristic of this hematologic malignancy, providing a targeted approach to disease control and highlighting the role of kinase inhibitors in modern leukemia treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2705
retrieved_docs.content: Bcr‑abl fusion proteins are linked to a state of deficiency, and this deficiency can specifically involve reduced levels of sulfur and testosterone. The presence of bcr‑abl fúzové proteiny, which are characteristic of certain hematologic malignancies, may therefore be associated with metabolic disturbances that lower both síra and testosteron, underscoring the importance of monitoring these substances in patients with bcr‑abl–positive disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1728
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology is a classification framework that incorporates a specific subset of biomarker concepts, including BCR‑ABL fusion proteins, the trace element selenium, sex steroid hormones, and the vitamin thiamine (thiamin). By grouping these diverse molecular and nutritional markers together, the terminology provides a structured reference for researchers and clinicians to identify and discuss biomarkers relevant to disease mechanisms, diagnostic testing, and therapeutic monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 676
retrieved_docs.content: The CTRP Biomarker Terminology encompasses a diverse set of concepts that are used to describe biomarkers within the portal, including the BCR‑ABL fusion proteins, the element selenium, human factor VIII, and the sex steroid hormones. These entities are all considered subset concepts of the CTRP Biomarker Terminology, reflecting the breadth of molecular and biochemical markers that can be catalogued for research and clinical interpretation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2018
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, and imatinib are classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As members of this targeted‑therapy class, each of these agents may be used to treat lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي). The relationship indicates that the BCR‑ABL inhibitor class encompasses dasatinib, its anhydrous form, and imatinib, all of which share the therapeutic indication of managing lymphoid leukemia, highlighting their role in targeted treatment strategies for this hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2814
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology is a classification system that encompasses specific biomarker concepts, notably the bcr‑abl fusion proteins and the human factor VIII protein, as included subset concepts within this terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2801
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology is a specialized classification system that incorporates the concept of bcr-abl fusion proteins as well as the concept of glukokortikoidy (glucocorticoids). By including bcr-abl fusion proteins, the terminology acknowledges the relevance of these oncogenic fusion proteins—commonly associated with chronic myeloid leukemia and other hematologic malignancies—as important biomarkers for disease detection, prognosis, and targeted therapy. The inclusion of glukokortikoidy reflects the significance of glucocorticoids, a class of steroid hormones that are widely used in clinical practice for their anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects and can also serve as biomarkers for therapeutic response and endocrine function. Together, these concepts illustrate the breadth of CTRP Biomarker Terminology in covering both molecular oncogenic markers and pharmacologically active hormone classes relevant to patient diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology is a specialized classification system that includes the concept of bcr-abl fúzové proteiny, the BCR‑ABL fusion proteins implicated in leukemogenesis, as well as the element selen, recognized for its antioxidant properties and potential role as a biomarker in various disease states. By grouping these distinct entities under a single terminology, CTRP Biomarker Terminology supports integrated research and clinical evaluation of both oncogenic protein markers and trace‑element biomarkers.
